questions.question: What is the primary advantage of a monorepo over a polyrepo for cross-project changes?
options: Each project can use a different programming language
A single commit can atomically update a shared library and all its consumers
CI pipelines run faster because there is only one repository
Access control is easier because everyone has the same permissions
questions.explanation: The defining advantage of monorepos is atomic commits — you can update a shared library and every project that depends on it in a single commit, avoiding version skew and coordinated multi-PR updates.

questions.question: In a polyrepo setup, Service A uses auth-lib v2.0 and Service B uses auth-lib v1.3. What problem does this illustrate?
options: The build graph problem
Sparse checkout limitations
Version skew — services run different versions of the same dependency
Hermetic build failure
questions.explanation: Version skew occurs when different services in a polyrepo pin different versions of a shared dependency, leading to inconsistent behavior and integration bugs.

questions.question: What does 'affected target detection' solve in a monorepo CI pipeline?
options: It ensures only authorized developers can merge changes
It reduces CI time by only building and testing projects reachable from changed files
It publishes packages to a registry automatically
It enforces code formatting rules across all projects
questions.explanation: Affected target detection computes the dependency graph starting from changed files and identifies which projects need to be rebuilt or retested, skipping unaffected projects and dramatically reducing CI time.

questions.question: Which monorepo tool is best suited for a polyglot codebase (Python + Go + Java + TypeScript) at a large organization?
options: Turborepo
Lerna
Bazel
Rush
questions.explanation: Bazel is language-agnostic and supports hermetic builds with remote execution, making it the right choice for large polyglot monorepos. Turborepo, Lerna, and Rush are focused on JavaScript/TypeScript ecosystems.

questions.question: A team of 5 developers building a SaaS product with 3 services sharing common auth and UI libraries should most likely choose:
options: Polyrepo — each service gets its own repo for isolation
Monorepo — the coordination cost of polyrepos is higher than the CI complexity of a monorepo at this scale
Hybrid with git submodules — maximum flexibility
No version control — just deploy direct to production
questions.explanation: Small teams with heavily shared code benefit from monorepos because the publish-consume cycle of polyrepos adds coordination overhead that exceeds the CI complexity of a well-managed monorepo.

questions.question: How does a CODEOWNERS file address the access control weakness of monorepos?
options: It encrypts directories so unauthorized developers cannot read the code
It defines required reviewers per directory, enforcing that the right team approves changes before merge
It automatically deletes old branches to reduce repo size
It publishes packages to a private registry
questions.explanation: CODEOWNERS maps file paths to teams who must review changes. This enforces governance without repository-level isolation, providing a middle ground between open access and full polyrepo separation.
